tagline: Film room intelligence at machine speed.

round_of_64.reasoning: Auburn's interior depth and Johni Broome's dominance. Kelvin Sampson built this roster to avoid first-round chaos.
Creighton's motion offense creates the exact spacing problems Louisville struggles to guard. 8-9 games break toward the better ball-movement team.
Michigan's length and athleticism overwhelm mid-majors in the first weekend. Dustin May's squad isn't ready for this pace.
Texas A&M's physicality is a mismatch against an Ivy League team. Wade Taylor controls pace from the jump.
Ole Miss has the defensive profile to slow North Carolina's transition game. Hubert Davis needs ball-screen setups Ole Miss denies.
Iowa State's switchable defense and Prohm's system is too organized for a 14-seed. Tamin Lippert closes this early.
Marquette's Big East-tested defense handles the Mountain West pace. Shaka Smart's team is built for exactly this matchup type.
Michigan State's experience and Tom Izzo's tournament pedigree. Bryant has no answer for the Spartans' physicality on the glass.
Duke's talent level is a tier above any 16-seed. Cooper Flagg has been in this moment before.
Baylor's guard play and half-court execution edges out Mississippi State in a coin-flip. Scott Drew knows how to set up a tournament run.
Oregon's athleticism is a wall for Liberty's system. Dana Altman's team is experienced in the first weekend crucible.
Arizona's frontcourt depth is a problem Akron can't solve. Caleb Love thrives in the opening round spotlight.
BYU's size advantage negates VCU's pressing style. Kevin Young's system survives the VCU pressure better than most.
Wisconsin's deliberate pace neutralizes Montana's athleticism. Greg Gard's system was built to grind down exactly this kind of opponent.
Saint Mary's WCC polish and deliberate offense handles Vanderbilt's up-tempo style. Randy Bennett's system is tournament-proven.
Alabama's transition offense is unstoppable at tournament pace. Nate Oats has this team primed for a deep run.
Houston's defensive intensity smothers 16-seeds in the first half. Kelvin Sampson's system is built on exactly this kind of game-opening lockdown.
Gonzaga's talent advantage edges Drake in the 8-9 toss-up. Mark Few's experience navigating these opening games is the difference.
Clemson's ACC-tested defense holds McNeese's transition game in check. Brad Brownell's team is built to survive upset bids.
Purdue's size is simply too much for a 13-seed. Zach Edey's replacement has kept the system going and this game isn't close.
Illinois's length and physicality edges Texas in a grinder. Brad Underwood's squad has the defensive versatility to survive the SEC transfer portal team.
Kentucky's talent level is insurmountable for a Sun Belt team. John Calipari's replacement keeps the system rolling through the first weekend.
UCLA's Pac-10 athleticism handles the Mountain West's physical style. Mick Cronin's team knows how to win ugly in tournament settings.
Tennessee's defensive profile is overwhelming for a 15-seed. Rick Barnes has this team peaking at exactly the right time.
Florida's length and Dusty May's system is a tournament-built machine. Norfolk State has no answer for the Gators' interior presence.
UConn's back-to-back championship muscle memory is real. Dan Hurley has built a program that knows how to win tournament games.
Memphis's athleticism is a problem for any 12-seed. Penny Hardaway's team thrives in the open floor that tournament pace creates.
Maryland's Big Ten experience is a significant advantage over a 13-seed. Kevin Willard's system handles the WCC-style game plan Grand Canyon runs.
Missouri's SEC-hardened defense edges Colorado State's Mountain West attack. Dennis Gates has this team ready for the physical style of play.
Texas Tech's defensive system is built to strangle mid-majors. Grant McCasland's squad picked up where Chris Beard left off.
Kansas's tradition and talent edge the SEC's portal product. Bill Self's tournament preparation gives the Jayhawks the edge in a tight game.
St. John's Big East pedigree and Rick Pitino's tournament savvy is too much for a 15-seed. This game is over by halftime.

round_of_32.reasoning: Auburn's size advantage is too much for Creighton's motion scheme. Johni Broome dominates the glass and shuts down the paint.
Texas A&M's physicality wins this game. Wade Taylor's guard play neutralizes Michigan's ball-handling advantage.
Iowa State's defensive system is built for exactly the Ole Miss challenge. Tamin Lippert's scoring creates spacing Iowa State struggles with, but Prohm's adjustments win.
Tom Izzo's second-weekend experience is the difference. Michigan State's physical defense takes away Marquette's spacing game.
Duke's talent is on another level even against a capable Baylor squad. Cooper Flagg closes this one out with 25 in a statement game.
Arizona's frontcourt depth creates matchup problems Oregon hasn't faced. Caleb Love controls the game from the perimeter.
Wisconsin's deliberate pace suffocates BYU's spread offense. Gard's system wins the possession battle decisively.
Alabama's athleticism overwhelms Saint Mary's deliberate style at tournament speed. Nate Oats exploits every transition opportunity.
Houston's defensive intensity is a wall even Gonzaga's offensive machine struggles to scale. Sampson holds Few's offense under 60 points.
Purdue's Big Ten-caliber post game is too much for Clemson's perimeter-oriented defense. The Boilermakers grind this one out.
Kentucky's talent level edges Illinois in the second weekend. The Wildcats' athleticism opens up driving lanes Illinois can't contain.
Tennessee's defensive intensity is the wall UCLA's offense can't climb. Rick Barnes' system thrives in second-weekend physical play.
Florida's length creates the exact problems UConn's wing-heavy attack struggles to solve. Dusty May's system wins the rebounding battle decisively.
Maryland's Big Ten experience beats Memphis's athleticism in a grinding second-round game. Willard's defensive schemes slow Penny's offense.
Texas Tech's defensive system shuts down Missouri's half-court offense. McCasland's team wins the turnover battle by double digits.
St. John's Big East depth and Rick Pitino's adjustment game is too much for Kansas in the second round. The guards control the game.

sweet_16.reasoning: Auburn's full-court pressure creates turnovers Texas A&M's guards can't handle. Broome's dominance on both ends is too much even for a physical SEC team.
Tom Izzo's Elite Eight preparation is the difference. Michigan State's physical defense clamps Iowa State's motion and Izzo outcoaches Prohm in late-game situations.
Duke's depth and Scheyer's game-planning defeats Arizona's perimeter-heavy offense. Cooper Flagg goes off and Arizona has no matchup answer.
Wisconsin's deliberate pace neutralizes Alabama's transition game — the one thing Oats can't weaponize. Gard wins the possession battle and the game.
Houston's defensive wall holds Purdue's post game to single digits in the paint. Sampson's pressure system forces a career-high turnover game for the Boilermakers.
Tennessee's battle-tested defense is a style problem for Kentucky's perimeter attack. Barnes adjusts at halftime and Tennessee pulls away in the final five minutes.
Florida's length creates the paint problems Maryland's offense can't solve. Dusty May's system is built for exactly this type of physical Sweet 16 grinder.
Texas Tech's defensive system clamps St. John's guard-heavy offense. McCasland's team forces Pitino into half-court sets that Texas Tech wins consistently.

elite_8.reasoning: Tom Izzo's Elite Eight record is elite for a reason. Michigan State's backcourt outperforms expectations and shuts down Auburn's transition — the one thing Izzo specifically gameplanned for.
Duke's talent advantage becomes decisive at the Elite Eight stage. Cooper Flagg's two-way impact forces Wisconsin into shot-clock violations that doom the deliberate offense.
Houston's defensive efficiency is the best in the country. Sampson's system holds Tennessee to a season-low scoring output in a classic lockdown performance.
Florida's size advantage is decisive against Texas Tech's perimeter-reliant offense. Dusty May proves this team is built for the Final Four stage.

final_four.reasoning: Jon Scheyer outcoaches Kelvin Sampson in a defensive battle. Duke's talent advantage forces Houston into a half-court game the Cougars don't win. Cooper Flagg takes over in the final two minutes.
Florida's size advantage is decisive. Dusty May's system creates paint touches Michigan State's interior defense can't stop. The Gators advance to their first title game in nearly 20 years.

championship.reasoning: Dusty May proves himself on the biggest stage. Florida's length creates matchup nightmares Duke hasn't seen all season. The Gators win their third national championship in a career-defining moment for May.
